metadata.visit_title: GI clinic — pre-procedure consult for diagnostic EGD, positive celiac serology and iron deficiency

planted_discrepancies.chart_state: referral notes gluten-containing diet confirmed ongoing
planted_discrepancies.spoken_truth: patient confirms still eating bread/pasta daily, has NOT started a gluten-free diet
planted_discrepancies.note: Critical pre-biopsy condition — duodenal biopsy for celiac must be done on a gluten-containing diet or it may be falsely negative. Confirmed true.

planted_discrepancies.chart_state: no anticoagulant/antiplatelet on file; no OTC supplements listed
planted_discrepancies.spoken_truth: patient says he takes a 'blood thinner' which on clarification is OTC fish oil (omega-3), not a true anticoagulant
planted_discrepancies.note: Classic trap: 'blood thinner' = fish oil. No true anticoagulant; no periprocedural hold required, but OTC supplement should be added to the med list.

planted_discrepancies.chart_state: problem list carries Celiac disease as an active/confirmed diagnosis
planted_discrepancies.spoken_truth: diagnosis is serology-suspected only; confirmatory duodenal biopsy is the reason for this visit and has not been done
planted_discrepancies.note: Serology-positive but histologically unconfirmed; diagnosis should read provisional/suspected until biopsy.

planted_discrepancies.spoken_truth: patient reports an older sister with biopsy-confirmed celiac disease
planted_discrepancies.note: Supportive family history not previously captured; strengthens pretest probability.

planted_discrepancies.chart_state: multivitamin with iron (MVI with iron) = active
planted_discrepancies.spoken_truth: patient confirms taking a daily multivitamin that contains iron
patient confirms no known drug allergies

prior_notes.text: 38-year-old man evaluated for several months of bloating, intermittent loose stools, and fatigue. CBC notable for mild microcytic anemia (Hgb 12.9) with low ferritin (18). Celiac serology sent: tissue transglutaminase IgA markedly elevated (>10x ULN) with normal total IgA. Findings strongly suggestive of celiac disease. Patient counseled to CONTINUE a normal gluten-containing diet and NOT to start gluten-free eating until after diagnostic endoscopy. Started on a daily multivitamin with iron for iron deficiency. Referred to Gastroenterology for confirmatory upper endoscopy with duodenal biopsy.

Tissue transglutaminase IgA antibody markedly elevated at greater than 10 times the upper limit of normal. Total serum IgA within normal limits (IgA-sufficient, result interpretable). CBC: Hgb 12.9 g/dL, MCV 78 fL. Ferritin 18 ug/L (low). Interpretation: serologic profile strongly supportive of celiac disease; tissue confirmation recommended.

Condition._discrepancy: D3: chart carries celiac as confirmed; diagnosis is serology-suspected pending duodenal biopsy

medicationCodeableConcept.text: multivitamin with iron oral product (MVI with iron)

coding.display: Tissue transglutaminase IgA Ab [Units/volume] in Serum

Tissue transglutaminase IgA Ab [Units/volume] in Serum

DiagnosticReport.conclusion: tTG-IgA markedly elevated (>10x ULN) with normal total IgA. Serologic profile strongly supportive of celiac disease; duodenal biopsy recommended for confirmation while on a gluten-containing diet.

transcript:
DR: Good morning, Mr. Fuentes. I'm one of the GI doctors — you were sent over because your blood work came back strongly pointing toward celiac disease, and we want to confirm it with a scope and a few small biopsies. Sound right?
MA: Before the doctor came in, I grabbed your vitals — height 5 foot 10, weight 176 pounds. Temp 36.8, heart rate 72, blood pressure 122 over 76, respirations 14, oxygen 98 percent on room air.
PT: Yes, that's what my regular doctor told me. My tests were really high, she said.
DR: They were — the celiac antibody was more than ten times the normal limit, so that's a strong signal. The way we confirm it for sure is an upper endoscopy where we take tiny samples from the top of your small intestine. Before that, one really important thing: have you changed how you eat? Cut out bread, wheat, that kind of thing?
PT: No, no. She told me to keep eating normally until the test, so I did. I still have toast in the morning, pasta a couple nights a week. Nothing changed.
DR: That's exactly what we needed to hear. If you'd gone gluten-free already, the biopsy could look normal even if you have it, and we'd be stuck. So please keep eating gluten right up until the procedure.
PT: Okay, got it. I'll keep eating my bread.
DR: Perfect. Let me check your medicines. I have a multivitamin with iron — for the low iron, right?
PT: Yes, one every morning.
DR: Anything else? Any blood thinners? That matters because we sometimes take biopsies.
PT: Well — I do take a blood thinner. The fish oil ones? My cousin said they thin the blood.
DR: Ah — fish oil, the omega-3 capsules. That's actually not a true blood thinner, so it won't be a problem for the biopsies, and you don't need to stop it. But I'm glad you mentioned it — I'll add it to your list. No prescription blood thinners like warfarin or aspirin daily?
PT: No, nothing like that.
DR: Any allergies to medicines?
PT: None that I know of.
DR: Good. One more — any family history of celiac?
PT: Actually yes, my older sister has it. She had a biopsy years ago.
DR: That's helpful, thank you. So the plan: schedule the scope with biopsies, keep eating gluten, keep the vitamin. The team will send prep instructions.
PT: Sounds good.
note:
**Subjective:** Mr. Fuentes is a 38-year-old man referred to GI for a pre-procedure consult and diagnostic esophagogastroduodenoscopy (EGD) with duodenal biopsy. Primary care evaluation for months of bloating, intermittent loose stools, and fatigue revealed markedly elevated tTG-IgA (>10x ULN) with normal total IgA, plus mild microcytic iron deficiency (Hgb 12.9, ferritin 18). He confirms he has continued a normal gluten-containing diet (daily toast, pasta several nights weekly) and has NOT started a gluten-free diet — essential for diagnostic yield of the biopsy. He takes a daily multivitamin with iron. On review of medications he reported a "blood thinner," which on clarification is over-the-counter fish oil (omega-3); he denies any prescription anticoagulant or antiplatelet (no warfarin, DOAC, or daily aspirin). No known drug allergies. He reports an older sister with biopsy-confirmed celiac disease.

**Objective:** Vitals (today): T 36.8 C, HR 72, RR 14, BP 122/76, SpO2 98% on RA; Ht 178 cm (5'10"), Wt 80 kg (176 lb), BMI 25.2. Labs (2026-06-20): tTG-IgA 152 U/mL (ref <15; >10x ULN), total IgA 214 mg/dL (normal, result interpretable), hemoglobin 12.9 g/dL (mildly low, microcytic), ferritin 18 ug/L (low). Well-appearing, no acute distress.

**Assessment & Plan:**
- Serology-suspected celiac disease (tTG-IgA >10x ULN, IgA-sufficient), histologically unconfirmed — proceed with diagnostic EGD and duodenal biopsies for confirmation. Update problem list to reflect suspected/provisional status pending pathology.
- Continue gluten-containing diet until after the procedure — reinforced with patient; do not initiate gluten-free eating pre-biopsy.
- Periprocedural anticoagulation: no true anticoagulant/antiplatelet. "Blood thinner" clarified as OTC fish oil (omega-3) — no hold required; add omega-3 to medication list.
- Iron deficiency (Hgb 12.9, ferritin 18) — continue multivitamin with iron; reassess iron studies after diagnosis; likely secondary to malabsorption if celiac confirmed.
- New family history: sister with biopsy-confirmed celiac — add to record.
- Schedule EGD with biopsy; bowel/pre-procedure and fasting instructions to follow.
after_visit_summary:
Visit summary

What we discussed
• Your blood test strongly suggests celiac disease
• We will confirm it with an upper endoscopy (EGD) and small biopsies
• Your low iron
• Your medicines and supplements

Next steps
• Keep eating your normal diet WITH gluten (bread, pasta) until after the endoscopy — this is important so the biopsy gives an accurate result. Do not start a gluten-free diet yet.
• We will schedule your endoscopy; the team will send fasting and prep instructions.
• Your fish oil is not a true blood thinner — you do not need to stop it. We added it to your record.
• Keep taking your daily multivitamin with iron.
• We noted your sister's celiac disease in your family history.
• Call if you have severe belly pain, vomiting, or black/bloody stools before the procedure.
